item.title: Vance Boelter gets 2 life terms and more in Minnesota lawmaker shootings
item.description: Vance Boelter, the man who admitted in court to shooting two Minnesota lawmakers and their spouses, was sentenced to two life terms in prison followed by 40 years Thursday morning.

item.title: U.S. launches 13th straight night of strikes on Iran
item.description: The U.S. on Thursday launched a 13th straight night of strikes against Iran after President Trump threatened Iran and the Houthis with "major military punishment." Retired Marine Col. Mark Cancian joins with analysis.

item.title: Secret Service agent on Vance detail under investigation for allegedly leaking information
item.description: The Secret Service confirms that an agent on Vice President JD Vance's security detail is being investigated for allegedly leaking information that compromised operational security. Criminal charges are possible. Nicole Sganga shares the details.

item.title: 25 states sue FEMA and DHS, accuse Trump admin. of withholding funding
item.description: The lawsuit claims the Trump administration is withholding hundreds of millions of dollars in disaster-preparedness and homeland security grants to pressure states to change policies on elections and immigration.

item.title: FDA says new cyclospora outbreak linked to unspecified product
item.description: Federal health officials said they've identified a new outbreak of the cyclospora parasite, but the source hasn't been specified.

item.title: Remembering cameraman Derek Williams, "the mate you would want in any war zone"
item.description: Derek Williams died Wednesday, according to his family. He had been living with Parkinson's disease and was recently diagnosed with cancer.

item.title: Wisconsin governor calls for accountability as protests over fatal police shooting continue
item.description: Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers is calling for accountability over the deadly police shooting of a man in Madison. Protests have erupted in the wake of the incident. CBS News' Jonah Kaplan has more.

item.title: Taylor Farms reportedly shut down Mexican processing facility linked to cyclospora outbreak
item.description: U.S. health officials said they are investigating an additional outbreak of cyclospora, but do not know its source. Plus, Taylor Farms has reportedly shut down the plant where lettuce is suspected to have started the initial outbreak that has sickened thousands in at least 41 states. Carter Evans reports.

item.title: Man shot and killed by police in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests
item.description: Madison's chief of police says an officer in Wisconsin's capital city shot and killed a man who had injured him with a knife while resisting arrest.

item.title: House rebukes Trump over Iran war, but similar war powers effort stalls in the Senate
item.description: The House on Thursday passed a measure to limit President Trump's wartime powers, reflecting negative sentiment around Iran. A similar measure was struck down in the Senate hours later. CBS News' Taurean Small reports.

item.title: U.S. launches more Iran strikes as Trump threatens "major military punishment"
item.description: The U.S. moves more refueling planes to the Mideast and adds medics at a major military hospital as the war with Iran spreads to another key waterway.

item.title: Latest on U.S. military buildup in the Middle East; Saudi Arabia nuclear deal
item.description: Sources say the United States military is building up its forces in the Middle East as President Trump warns that it will hold Iran accountable for any Houthi attacks. Meanwhile, Saudi Arabia has yet to respond after President Trump said the Saudis would have to join the Abraham Accords in order to finalize the U.S.-Saudi nuclear deal. CBS News' Kathryn Watson breaks it all down.

item.title: 2 men stabbed in New York City, police investigating possible hate crime
item.description: Two men were stabbed on Thursday near Central Park in New York City, just blocks apart. The victims are Asian and Jewish, and the attacks are being investigated as a possible hate crime. Tom Hanson reports.

item.title: Protesters take the mic at Wisconsin police shooting press conference
item.description: A day after an officer shot and killed a man in Madison, Wisconsin, police say the man had fled from officers, then pulled a fixed-blade knife, injuring one officer, who tried a taser before firing three shots. Protests developed almost immediately, and on Thursday, demonstrators took the microphone in a police press conference. Jonah Kaplan reports.

item.title: Remembering legendary CBS News cameraman Derek Williams
item.description: Derek Williams, who worked as a cameraman for decades at CBS News, has died at 79. CBS News congressional correspondent Caitlin Huey-Burns looks back at his storied career.

item.title: New poll shows Democrats with 11-point lead on generic ballot
item.description: Emerson College has found that on a generic ballot, Democrats hold an 11-point lead over Republicans, 53% to 42%, heading into the midterm elections. Political strategists Mark Bednar and Mo Elleithee join with analysis.

item.title: ChatGPT solved cybersecurity task that typically takes weeks in about 8 hours, expert says
item.description: New research from a cybersecurity company now indicates that artificial intelligence models are getting better at solving multi-part problems. Gabriel Bernadett-Shapiro, who worked on the project and is a distinguished AI research scientist at SentinelOne, joins to unpack his team's research.

item.title: Former DNC Chair Jaime Harrison makes his case for keeping South Carolina as first 2028 primary
item.description: The debate over Democrats' 2028 primary calendar has gotten contentious, pitting those who want South Carolina to be first against those who want Nevada. Jaime Harrison, former Democratic National Committee chair, joins with his perspective.

item.title: Rescheduled White House Correspondents' Dinner will have tighter security, fewer guests
item.description: The Secret Service is tightening security ahead of Friday's rescheduled White House Correspondents' Dinner. The original dinner in April was shut down after a gunman opened fire as he rushed through a screening checkpoint. CBS News senior White House correspondent Weijia Jiang, president of the White House Correspondents' Association, joins to discuss.

item.title: GOP House Judiciary chair issues criminal referral against Jack Smith
item.description: Former special counsel Jack Smith, who attempted to prosecute then-former President Trump for his actions after the 2020 election, may now become a target of the Justice Department. CBS News homeland security correspondent Nicole Sganga has the details.

item.title: The implications of the U.S.-Saudi Arabia nuclear deal
item.description: After signing a nuclear accord with Saudi Arabia, President Trump says that the agreement hinges on the Saudis establishing diplomatic relations with Israel, which they have refused to do. CBS News chief foreign affairs correspondent and "Face the Nation" moderator Margaret Brennan has the latest. Then, former CIA Deputy Assistant Director for Counterterrorism Joe Zacks joins with analysis.

item.title: Judge temporarily halts Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery merger
item.description: The ruling comes after a coalition of 12 states sued to block the deal, arguing it would harm consumers and the entertainment industry.

item.title: Trump tells Axios he's considering a "massive attack" on Iran "bigger than ever before"
item.description: A major escalation in the Iran war may be coming, with President Trump telling Axios on Thursday he's considering a "massive attack." CBS News chief White House correspondent Nancy Cordes has the details.

item.title: Trump imposes new tariffs on dozens of countries, citing forced labor concerns
item.description: The Trump administration will impose tariffs of up to 12.5% on goods from 60 U.S. trading partners accused of failing to crack down on forced labor, extending the administration's tariffs on much of the world.

item.title: This week on "Sunday Morning" (July 26)
item.description: A look at the features for this week's broadcast of the Emmy-winning program, hosted by Jane Pauley.

item.title: Rapid pace of interceptor and precision-guided weapons use worries Trump admin.
item.description: The U.S. defense industrial base isn't equipped to produce missiles rapidly enough to replenish munitions used in the war with Iran, or transferred for use to Central Command.

item.title: FDA panel considers loosening regulations for some peptides
item.description: The FDA panel will review seven peptides and decide if they should be added to a list of substances that can be safely produced by compounding pharmacies.

item.title: Fewer Americans own homes than thought, new Fed search finds
item.description: Only about 53% of Americans own homes, or about 12 percentage points lower than previous data has shown, the study found.

item.title: U.S. and Saudis sign nuclear deal, contingent on joining Abraham Accords
item.description: President Trump said on Thursday the deal would include no enrichment of nuclear material.

item.title: Malcolm-Jamal Warner's widow on actor's death: "My body went through shock"
item.description: Tenisha Warner, the widow of "The Cosby Show" actor Malcolm-Jamal Warner, spoke exclusively to "CBS Mornings" co-host Gayle King about coping with grief one year after his death.
